comment_text:
This should have been soooo much better! I absolutely love this genre (the "Before" trilogy, "Once," "Me Before You," etc.) -- it has a brilliant/beautiful/talented cast, ridiculously stunning location, Netflix money, etc.  But it faltered in the edit bay and the lack of a good score.  The editing is atrocious.  The scenes have multiple, jarring jump cuts, they have a natural ending shot but then keep going back in (no spoilers; like the scene at the end with the dad and the family at that location in the lobby, it was done, then comes back for a three shot), and the whole edit of film was such an odd, clumsy rhythm.  (Yes, I'm an editor for a living, for a different studio, but all love with Netflix.)  The intro scenes and act one where they meet and all in love was super, super rushed where you don't get to know them at all, just that since they're the stars, you should root for them... then a forced montage where, boom, they're together so the story can happen. When that happened, I knew what the ending was because then you knew it wasn't about "will they, won't they" -- it became (ya know, about 'that' ending).  The second act is incredibly long and spins its tires, jamming in way too many subplots and the friends' relationships the didn't lead anywhere, you didn't care about any of them (even though they seemed lovable).  They didn't do a good job of letting you root for these characters or why they needed to be in love.  His character is all over the place; calm, pragmatic, then RAGE... then calm, then smart, etc.  It's not consistent. She has no arc because you don't feel for a second that she would be interested in her career path, there were no stakes for her to take that path so when she 'had to choose', it wasn't a hard choice.

Also, what in the world was with the score!? It was just like 'needle drop' library music that didn't match.  There was no weight to the music at the low moments.  They got some great licensed music, like Coldplay, but nothing was working again, because of the editing.

A HUGE, amazing plus was the audio. The audio on them, even in wind, was absolute perfection, like they were recording in a sound booth even when you could see how windy it was.  

I really, really, really wanted to love this film... I promise.  I loved Oxford, was just there a couple months ago, walked in almost every location they were.  It was just a mess in the edit but there is a great film in here.

This film was very beautiful ❤️ and fulfilling.
It is for young maturing adults, the target audience changed as Sofia is an adult. Learn how to appreciate slower situations: it is a ROM com drama movie. Not a sci-fi thing.


Anyway, the target audience was older, at least 24. I mean it is about an MA student who has an affair with the professor. Only few people attend a master's degree in the anglosphere which is radically different than most of the countries in Europe. If you do pursue a Master's degree in the UK or the US, you are usually around 25 minimum. After turning 25 you will go back to it, trust me :).
I fondly loved it ❤

comment_text: Love Queen Charlotte/George has got a new vocation…but as a teacher it really grinds my gears when these dramatisations come out about teacher-student relationships! video_title: My Oxford Year | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I wished to be in a good foreign university, have fun have friends, go on parties, travel foreign land with friends, date beautiful white girls, learn new things, that energetic college life, brag about it, maybe fall in love, maybe marry there or maybe not.... At first I couldn't afford it, never drown my family in loans, never meritorious enough to get scholarship because memorization & repetition is not my thing... Then I tried again years later but came pandemic... never wanted to go for certificates degree but for living a good life, memories, fun..what the media, screens always bombard us with that dream of good college life...I know where I am, I am good, married , with children & in a good state..& not too much greedy for more but I feel sad that this desire could not be fulfilled :/ Even if I could re-try will I be young enough? & I am a family guy, I can't hurt anyone....this thing that I wished for so much could not be true. I believe it happened for better. Feel sad but it's better, I believe. I trust the universe & everything that creates life.

video_title: My Oxford Year | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: Every time, it's the same story, a girl sets out to study, to build a future, and then she falls in love. And of course, the guy she falls for is emotionally broken. Suddenly, her ambitions fade into the background as she takes on the role of his personal therapist, sacrificing her dreams to help him heal. Why are men always portrayed as the ones who are shattered, while women are expected to fix them? Why is a woman’s growth always sidelined for the sake of a man’s healing? And for once, why don’t we see a story where the girl is the broken one, and the guy stays, supports her, helps her rebuild? But no, that’s never shown as “realistic,” because in reality, most men don’t stay. They leave, in search of someone who isn’t “too much to handle.”

comment_text:
While it is all intriguing, and definitely promises to carry itself to a profoundly moving, and a heartfelt climax, it gets disturbing to see when large scale productions normalise desecrating the sanctity of a mentor-mentee, or any form of pedagogical relationship, thereby uprooting entire societal foundations of respect, and setting entirely new but twisted precedents, the "new normale", of how further teacher-student dimensions would operate. Where it previously existed on covertly produced taboo genres, the fact that it's gone mainstream, bodes horribly for us.

My even bothering with such lengthy passages, is solely due to my parallel concerns over how addictive screentimes and ai have the possibilty of proven brain damage, lessening attention spans and memories, and it's all due to the manipulative monopoly of capitalists, destroying both previous and upcoming generations, which the inherent basis of this film that its production is advocating through, also has the potential of doing.
